title: Flapjacks
subtitle: Oat-and-golden-syrup traybake
excerpt: A proper British flapjack: chewy in the centre, slightly crisp at the edge, held together by golden syrup and butter with no flour or egg. Twenty minutes of effort, thirty minutes in the oven.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932) — the oat-and-syrup traybake as a regional British bake. The specific flapjack formula is documented in interwar British cookery columns; Mrs Beeton's 1861 edition has cognate oat biscuit recipes (Project Gutenberg #10136) that use the same method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap individual squares in baking paper, freeze in a bag. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps well for 5 days in a tin. Freezes for up to 3 months.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thick, amber-coloured invert sugar syrup made from refining cane sugar. It does not crystallise at room temperature, which keeps flapjacks chewy rather than grainy. Not the same as corn syrup or maple syrup.

content.text: The difference between a flapjack and a granola bar is

. It is the binding agent, the flavour backbone, and the reason a flapjack stays chewy rather than going granular. Use Lyle's Black Treacle in place of a quarter of the syrup if you want a darker, more caramelised result.

Two things determine the texture: the ratio of syrup to butter, and how long it stays in the oven. More syrup gives a chewier result; more time gives a crispier one. This recipe is calibrated for the middle ground: chewy with crisp edges.
Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease a 20×20 cm tin and line with baking paper, leaving an overhang on two sides for lifting.

Put the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{light-brown-sugar}}, {{golden-syrup}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}} in a medium saucepan over low heat. Stir until the butter has melted and the sugar has dissolved. Do not let the mixture boil. Remove from the heat.
Add the {{rolled-oats}} and stir until every oat is coated. Tip into the tin and press down firmly with the back of a spoon or your damp hands. The mixture should be evenly compressed and level.

Bake for 22–25 minutes. The flapjack is done when it is golden brown all over and the edges have pulled from the tin. The centre will still feel slightly soft; it firms up as it cools. Do not overbake.
Score the flapjack into 16 squares while it is hot, using a sharp knife. The scoring lines define where it will break when cold. Leave to cool completely in the tin before lifting out and separating.
